era_context: Late 14th century: Ottoman Empire consolidating Balkan power; Ming Dynasty under Zhu Yuanzhang in mature phase; Goryeo Dynasty in final decline; Timur's empire at its height; Western Schism ongoing in Catholic Church; English political tensions following the Peasants' Revolt (1381).
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islands and Oceania (no contemporary records)
Southeast Asia (limited European documentation; local records sparse)

events.title: Murad I consolidates Ottoman control in the Balkans
events.description: Ottoman Sultan Murad I continued his systematic conquest and consolidation of Balkan territories. In 1387, he was strengthening Ottoman administrative control over recently conquered Serbian and Bulgarian lands. His military campaigns were establishing Ottoman dominance in the region, setting the stage for the pivotal Battle of Kosovo Polje in 1389. Murad's reign (1362–1389) represented the transformation of the Ottoman state from a regional power to a major imperial force.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion during Murad I's reign is well-documented, but specific events in 1387 are not individually detailed. This entry reflects the documented pattern of Ottoman military and administrative activity during this period.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's administrative reforms in Ming China
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu Emperor) was implementing comprehensive administrative reforms to centralize Ming power and eliminate potential rivals. In 1387, he was consolidating the examination system, reorganizing provincial administration, and purging officials deemed disloyal. These reforms established the bureaucratic foundations of the Ming Dynasty that would persist for centuries. The year 1387 represents the mature phase of his administrative consolidation.
events.certainty_note: Zhu Yuanzhang's administrative reforms are well-documented in Ming official records. The year 1387 falls within his documented period of systematic bureaucratic reorganization.

events.title: Goryeo Dynasty in terminal decline
events.description: The Goryeo Dynasty was in its final years, with political authority fragmenting among competing factions. In 1387, the court was weakened by internal conflicts and the rising power of military generals like Yi Seong-gye. The year 1387 represents the penultimate phase of Goryeo's rule before Yi's coup in 1388 and the formal founding of Joseon in 1392. Goryeo's decline was marked by loss of territorial control and administrative coherence.

sources.name: Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Annals of the Joseon Kings)

events.certainty_note: Goryeo's decline in the late 14th century is well-documented in Korean historical records. The year 1387 falls clearly within the documented period of Goryeo's terminal decline.

events.title: Timur's military campaigns in the Caucasus
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) was conducting military campaigns in the Caucasus region, consolidating control over the territories between Central Asia and Anatolia. In 1387, he was strengthening his empire's borders and eliminating rival powers. His campaigns were establishing Timurid dominance over the region and setting conditions for his later campaigns into the Levant and Egypt (1400–1401).

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafar-nama (Book of Victories)
Ibn Arabshah, Aja'ib al-Maqdur (Wonders of Destiny)

events.certainty_note: Timur's general military campaigns are well-documented, but specific events in 1387 are not precisely dated in surviving sources. The year falls within his documented period of Caucasian operations.

events.title: English political tensions and Richard II's consolidation
events.description: In England, King Richard II (r. 1377–1399) was consolidating his authority following the Peasants' Revolt (1381). In 1387, he was facing opposition from the nobility, particularly the 'Lords Appellant' who would challenge his authority in 1388. The year 1387 represents the prelude to the political crisis that would dominate 1388–1389 and eventually lead to Richard's deposition in 1399.
key_figures: Thomas of Woodstock, Duke of Gloucester (Lord Appellant)

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: Richard II's reign and the political tensions of the late 1380s are well-documented in English royal records and contemporary chronicles. The year 1387 is clearly established as the prelude to the Lords Appellant crisis.

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of Murad I's Balkan campaigns as a systematic 'conquest' may overstate Ottoman military superiority and understate the role of internal Balkan political fragmentation. Recent scholarship suggests that Ottoman expansion was as much a result of Balkan Christian states' internal conflicts as of Ottoman military prowess. Additionally, the narrative of Ottoman 'conquest' carries implicit assumptions about inevitability that modern historians question. The Hongwu Emperor's administrative reforms are often portrayed as purely centralizing, but recent scholarship emphasizes the persistence of regional power and the limits of centralization even under Zhu Yuanzhang's strong rule.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1387 is moderate but regionally uneven. East Asian sources (Ming Shilu, Goryeo-sa) are contemporary or near-contemporary. Ottoman and Central Asian sources are primarily later compilations. English sources are contemporary and detailed. There is a Western bias in available documentation, with Ottoman, Central Asian, and East Asian sources receiving less attention in Western historiography. The year 1387 is less well-documented than 1389 (Battle of Kosovo) or 1392 (Joseon founding), suggesting that historical attention clusters around dramatic events. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania have no documented written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Murad I's consolidation in 1387 set conditions for the Battle of Kosovo Polje in 1389
Hongwu Emperor's administrative reforms in 1387 established the Ming system that would continue after his death
Goryeo's decline in 1387 directly preceded Yi Seong-gye's coup in 1388
Political tensions in 1387 led to the Merciless Parliament and Lords Appellant crisis in 1388
